course_title: Two-Phase Direct Liquid Cooling Efficiencies and Fluids
course_subtitle: Efficient phase-change cooling, dielectric fluids, and operational practices for high-density infrastructure.

sources.title: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks
2026 OCP EMEA Summit panel: Revolutionizing AI Heat Management - The Superior Efficiency of Two-Phase Liquid Cooling
OCP Educational Webinar Program - past webinars

term_glossary.tooltip: Direct liquid cooling removes heat at or near IT components through a liquid cooling loop.
Technology Cooling System: the secondary loop that serves IT equipment and transfers heat to the CDU boundary.
Facility Water System: the primary facility loop that receives heat from the CDU and rejects it upstream.
Coolant Distribution Unit: the boundary equipment that connects IT cooling loops to facility heat rejection.
Quick disconnect: a connector used to service or connect liquid cooling components while controlling fluid release.
Universal quick disconnect work supports more reusable, interoperable liquid-cooling infrastructure.
Ozone Depletion Potential is an environmental metric; the OCP guideline calls for zero ODP.
Global Warming Potential compares climate impact; the OCP guideline sets a low-GWP selection expectation.
Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances are a regulatory and sustainability concern for some fluid roadmaps.
Safety Data Sheet: the handling, exposure, storage, and response reference for the selected fluid.
Personal protective equipment such as eye protection, gloves, and protective clothing used during fluid handling.
All materials that directly contact the heat-transfer fluid, including seals, pumps, heat exchangers, QDs, and manifolds.
A compatibility screening method used in the OCP guidance before longer functional testing.
Gases such as air that do not condense in the operating range and can degrade two-phase performance.
At saturation, pressure and boiling temperature are linked, so pressure control helps control boiling behavior.
Cooling liquid below its saturation point, often important at pump inlet conditions in pumped two-phase designs.
A heat-flux limit where boiling behavior can change and thermal margin can be lost if the design is pushed too far.
A heat-rejection mode that uses favorable ambient conditions to reduce or avoid mechanical cooling.
Using recovered IT heat for another purpose when temperature, controls, and site integration make it practical.

source_refs.source: Internal course synthesis

slides.title: AI Racks Are Changing the Cooling Problem
slides.subtitle: The cooling discussion is moving from room averages to heat flux, rack density, and site constraints.

bullets.text: Accelerated systems concentrate more heat into smaller areas, so chip and rack cooling limits arrive earlier.
Air and rear-door approaches remain useful, but their practical envelope narrows as rack power and chip heat flux rise.
The value case depends on IT thermal demand, facility power, water, space, service model, and risk posture.
Phase change lets the technology loop move large heat loads with less circulating liquid than a comparable sensible-heat loop.

source_refs.source: OCP Educational Webinar transcript and slides
Internal course synthesis

figure.alt: Nested heat transfer loops from chip to heat rejection
figure.caption: Two-phase DLC changes the technology cooling loop while the upstream facility loop still rejects the same heat load.

slides.title: The Efficiency Levers to Track
slides.subtitle: Separate the mechanisms so claims can be tested one by one.

cards.title: Latent heat transport
cards.body: Boiling carries heat through phase change rather than only warming a liquid.

cards.title: Lower liquid flow
cards.body: Less circulating liquid can reduce pump burden and fluid inventory in the TCS.

cards.title: Warmer rejection
cards.body: Higher useful temperatures can widen economizer and heat-reuse opportunities.

cards.title: Shorter heat path
cards.body: Cold plates move heat near the chip instead of relying on high server airflow.

cards.title: Waterless in server
cards.body: Dielectric fluids avoid water inside the IT equipment loop.

cards.title: Facility reuse
cards.body: The FWS can often keep familiar heat-rejection roles at the CDU boundary.

source_refs.source: OCP Educational Webinar transcript and slides
2026 OCP EMEA Summit two-phase cooling panel transcript
Internal course synthesis

slides.title: Cooling Choices Will Coexist
slides.subtitle: Two-phase DLC is a fit-for-purpose option, not a universal replacement for every cooling method.

cards.title: Air cooling
cards.body: Mature and simple where rack density and room airflow remain sufficient.

cards.title: Rear-door heat exchanger
cards.body: Extends air-cooled rack envelopes by capturing heat at the rack rear.

cards.title: Single-phase DLC
cards.body: Familiar liquid loop using sensible heat, often the first direct-to-chip step.

cards.title: Pumped two-phase DLC
cards.body: Circulates dielectric fluid and returns vapor for condensation.

cards.title: Passive two-phase paths
cards.body: Can reduce mechanical pumping where loop design supports natural circulation.

cards.title: Immersion
cards.body: A different service model with high heat removal potential and different operations.

source_refs.source: 2026 OCP EMEA Summit two-phase cooling panel transcript
OCP Educational Webinar transcript and slides

slides.title: Adoption Is Triggered by Constraints
slides.subtitle: The decision is strongest when multiple constraints stack together.

bullets.text: Rack power and chip heat flux can make traditional airflow and high-flow liquid loops harder to scale.
Existing buildings may lack space, floor loading, pipe capacity, or water access for large infrastructure changes.
Cooling overhead can compete directly with useful compute capacity.
Teams need the right service procedures, monitoring, safety practices, and supplier ecosystem before broad rollout.

resource_callout.title: Industry discussion
resource_callout.text: The OCP EMEA panel recording adds practitioner perspectives on when two-phase cooling is attractive.

source_refs.source: OCP Educational Webinar transcript and slides
2026 OCP EMEA Summit two-phase cooling panel transcript

slides.title: Why the Timing Matters
items: Two-phase DLC becomes compelling when heat flux, rack density, site power, water, and service constraints converge.
The facility loop still matters; the CDU boundary connects the new technology loop to familiar heat rejection.
Efficiency claims should be traced to mechanisms: phase change, flow, pumping, rejection temperature, and operations.
The right cooling choice depends on workload, site constraints, and operator readiness.

source_refs.source: Internal course synthesis

slides.title: Check Your Readiness Framing
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains why two-phase DLC is being considered for high-density AI deployments?

choices.text: It eliminates the need for any facility heat-rejection equipment.
It uses latent heat and lower TCS-side flow to remove heat closer to dense chips when air and high-flow single-phase loops become less practical.
It is required for every data center, regardless of workload or rack density.
It replaces the facility water system with server fans.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Two-phase DLC still rejects heat, but phase change and lower TCS-side liquid flow can help dense systems.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Two-phase DLC still needs heat rejection. Its value comes from phase change and lower liquid flow near dense components.

questions.prompt: Which constraints can push brownfield or edge sites toward advanced liquid cooling choices?

choices.text: Limited space or floor loading for large cooling infrastructure.
Strict power budgets where cooling overhead competes with compute.
Limited water availability for evaporative cooling.
Unmanned sites that need lower-touch reliability.
A requirement that all sites use immersion tanks.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Space, power, water, and maintenance constraints can all shift the cooling choice.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The course treats two-phase as fit-for-purpose. Space, power, water, and maintenance constraints are the drivers.

modules.title: Two-Phase DLC Architecture and Efficiency Mechanics
modules.subtitle: Phase-change mechanics, loop boundaries, pressure control, and scale requirements.

slides.title: Two-Phase DLC Architecture and Efficiency Mechanics
slides.subtitle: How phase change, pressure control, CDU boundaries, and interoperability shape the technology loop.

slides.title: Deployment Choices You Will Classify
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to:
objectives: Distinguish sensible-heat and latent-heat cooling mechanisms.
Trace a controlled two-phase loop from liquid feed to vapor return and condensation.
Explain how pressure control relates to boiling temperature.
Identify where CDU, FWS, flow, pressure loss, and interoperability decisions affect scale.

slides.title: Sensible Heat vs. Latent Heat
slides.subtitle: Single-phase loops warm liquid. Two-phase loops use boiling and condensation as part of the heat transport mechanism.

bullets.text: A liquid absorbs heat by rising in temperature while staying in one phase.
A fluid absorbs heat while changing phase, then releases it when vapor condenses.
Two-phase loop pressure and boiling temperature are linked, so controls must manage both.
The loop still needs wetting, vapor removal, condensation, and stable operating margins.

figure.caption: The cold plate, vapor return, condenser, receiver, and pump or lift path work as one pressure-controlled thermal system.
figure.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 980 450" style="width:100%;max-width:980px;height:auto;display:block;" role="img" aria-label="Controlled two-phase cooling loop from receiver to cold plates to condenser">

slides.title: Pool Boiling Targets Dynamic Hot Spots

figure.caption: Boiling is useful only when liquid supply, wetted structures, and vapor removal stay controlled at the heat source.

slides.title: The CDU Is the Boundary Between Complexity and Reuse
slides.subtitle: At the rack edge, facility integration can remain familiar even when the technology loop is two-phase.
rows: Liquid absorbs sensible heat and returns warmer.
Liquid evaporates at cold plates and returns vapor for condensation.
Pumps and heat exchanger move heat to the FWS.
Condenser, receiver, pressure control, and heat exchange manage phase change.
Receives heat through the CDU and rejects it upstream.
Receives heat through the CDU and rejects it upstream.
Flow rate, pressure drop, water quality, and thermal margin.
Pressure, liquid inventory, vapor return, purge, and compatibility evidence.

source_refs.source: Internal course synthesis
Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf
OCP Educational Webinar transcript and slides

slides.title: Flow, Line Sizing, and Pressure Loss
slides.subtitle: Lower liquid flow does not remove the need for careful hydraulic design.

bullets.text: Source examples compare about 0.3 L/min with 1.5 L/min per 1,000 W, roughly an 80 percent liquid reduction.
Low liquid flow can coexist with larger vapor paths, so feed and return line sizing are not symmetric.
The OCP guideline maintains evaporator-to-condenser pressure loss below 0.2 bar; panel discussion cites about 0.1 to 0.2 bar.
Where a pump receives liquid, inlet conditions must avoid flashing and loss of pump performance.

source_refs.source: 2026 OCP EMEA Summit two-phase cooling panel transcript
OCP Educational Webinar transcript and slides
Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf

slides.title: Interoperability Is the Practical Scale Story
slides.subtitle: The technical loop only scales when it fits a broader multi-vendor infrastructure model.

bullets.text: Quick disconnects, manifolds, and rack plumbing need repeatable expectations across suppliers.
Fluid compatibility depends on every wetted part, not only the cold plate.
FWS and rack-side decisions should avoid forcing a bespoke facility design for every server option.
Standardized connector and validation work reduces risk as deployments move from pilots to fleet scale.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf
2026 OCP EMEA Summit two-phase cooling panel transcript
OCP Educational Webinar transcript and slides

slides.title: Architecture Mechanics to Carry Forward
items: Two-phase DLC uses boiling and condensation, so pressure, temperature, wetting, and vapor return are linked.
The CDU contains much of the added phase-change complexity while the FWS still rejects heat upstream.
Lower liquid flow can reduce burden, but line sizing, vapor paths, subcooling, and pressure loss still need engineering.
Interoperability and material compatibility are scale requirements, not finishing touches.

source_refs.source: Internal course synthesis

slides.title: Check Your Loop Mental Model
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: In a two-phase cold-plate loop, why does pressure control become a central control problem?

choices.text: Saturation pressure helps set the boiling temperature at the cold plate.
Pressure control changes the facility water into refrigerant.
Pressure control is only needed for air-cooled racks.
Pressure control replaces the need for material compatibility testing.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. In a saturated two-phase loop, pressure and boiling temperature are tied together.
questions.feedback_incorrect: In a saturated two-phase loop, pressure and boiling temperature are tied together, so pressure control stabilizes thermal behavior.

questions.prompt: Which design checks belong in the two-phase TCS path between cold plate and CDU?

choices.text: Keep pressure loss low enough for stable vapor return and condensation.
Account for liquid feed and vapor return line sizing differences.
Maintain subcooling where a pump must receive liquid.
Ignore QDs, connectors, and manifolds because vapor pressure solves all flow issues.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Two-phase can reduce liquid flow, but vapor-side and connector design still matter.
questions.feedback_incorrect: Two-phase can reduce liquid flow, but vapor return, connectors, manifolds, and pressure-loss budget still need deliberate design.

source_refs.source: Internal course synthesis

modules.title: Dielectric Fluids, Selection, and Compatibility
modules.subtitle: Fluid properties, wetted materials, safety, and environmental roadmaps.

slides.title: Dielectric Fluids, Selection, and Compatibility
slides.subtitle: How fluid properties, wetted materials, safety practice, and sustainability shape responsible two-phase DLC.

slides.title: Fluid Choices You Will Evaluate
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to:
objectives: Describe why dielectric heat-transfer fluid selection is central to two-phase DLC.
Use the OCP guideline property envelope, including the corrected boiling-point range.
Explain wetted-material compatibility and qualification evidence.
Connect safety, handling, GWP, ODP, PFAS, and lifecycle expectations.

slides.title: What Makes the Fluid Different
slides.subtitle: The fluid is not just a heat carrier; it is part of the electrical, mechanical, environmental, and service model.

bullets.text: The fluid is selected for use near electronics, so electrical safety is part of the design premise.
Boiling and condensation happen inside the technology loop, so the fluid must match the desired pressure-temperature range.
The same fluid contacts metals, elastomers, plastics, seals, pumps, heat exchangers, manifolds, and QDs.
Handling, reuse, refurbishment, analysis, and disposal need to be part of the operational plan.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf
OCP Educational Webinar transcript and slides

slides.title: Property Envelope from the OCP Guidelines
slides.subtitle: Use the corrected boiling-point range: -26.3 degrees C to 60 degrees C.
rows: Supports safe handling and data-center use.
Aligns two-phase operation with practical equipment temperatures.
Low toxicity and no flame propagation classification.
Preserves margin for transport and storage.
Vapor below 20 mW/mK; liquid below 80 mW/mK at 25 degrees C

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf

slides.title: Compatibility Is a Wetted-Material System Decision
slides.subtitle: Compatibility belongs to the full system, not to a generic fluid label.

bullets.text: Cold plates, pumps, heat exchangers, connectors, seals, hoses, manifolds, and service fittings are all part of the wetted-material set.
The OCP guidance places responsibility on compatibility evidence for the chosen fluid and system materials.
Adding or changing a wetted material requires re-evaluation for the same fluid and operating conditions.
Fleet decisions need records, tests, and traceable materials of construction.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf
OCP Educational Webinar transcript and slides

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf

slides.title: Safety Starts with SDS, PPE, and Handling Discipline
slides.subtitle: The fluid may be designed for data centers, but it still deserves disciplined handling.

bullets.text: The Safety Data Sheet defines hazards, handling, storage, exposure response, and disposal guidance for the selected fluid.
Safety glasses or goggles, impervious gloves, and protective clothing are typical handling controls.
Keep fluid, tools, and opened components clean and sealed to protect loop reliability.
Storage, transport, reuse, refurbishment, and disposal must follow supplier guidance and local rules.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf

slides.title: Sustainability and Fluid Roadmaps
slides.subtitle: Responsible selection weighs today's properties and tomorrow's regulatory direction.

bullets.text: The OCP guideline sets environmental expectations alongside thermal and safety properties.
Some fluid families face regulatory scrutiny, so roadmap transparency is part of procurement risk.
Future fluids may improve environmental profiles, but operators still need qualification evidence for each material set.
Reuse, refurbishment, analysis, and disposal records make sustainability claims auditable.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf
2026 OCP EMEA Summit two-phase cooling panel transcript
OCP Educational Webinar transcript and slides

slides.title: Fluid Decisions That Scale
items: Two-phase DLC fluid selection is a combined thermal, electrical, safety, compatibility, and environmental decision.
The OCP guideline property envelope includes the corrected boiling-point range of -26.3 degrees C to 60 degrees C.
Compatibility evidence follows the chosen fluid and the full wetted-material list.
SDS, PPE, cleanliness, lifecycle records, and sustainability roadmaps should be designed into operations early.

source_refs.source: Internal course synthesis

slides.title: Check Your Fluid Decisions
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: Which properties are part of the OCP guideline's dielectric fluid selection envelope?

choices.text: Low toxicity and non-flammability.
Low global warming potential and zero ozone depletion potential.
ASHRAE Std. 34 A1 safety classification.
Ability to support any elastomer without testing.
Thermal properties suitable for the chosen two-phase operating range.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Safety, environmental, and thermal properties matter, while compatibility still requires testing.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The selection envelope includes safety, environmental, and thermal expectations, but no fluid automatically supports every material.

questions.prompt: What should happen if a new wetted material is added to the system?

choices.text: Nothing, as long as the fluid is dielectric.
Re-evaluate compatibility for the specific fluid and wetted-material list.
Replace the facility water system.
Disable all monitoring until the next IT refresh.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Compatibility evidence must match the specific fluid and wetted-material list.
questions.feedback_incorrect: A new wetted material changes the compatibility question and should be re-evaluated for the chosen fluid.

source_refs.source: Internal course synthesis

slides.thank_you_message: Nice work. You've finished Module 3: Dielectric Fluids, Selection, and Compatibility.

modules.title: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, and Lifecycle
modules.subtitle: Startup, purge, monitoring, maintenance, deployment patterns, and records.

slides.title: Deployment, Operations, Monitoring, and Lifecycle
slides.subtitle: Turning two-phase DLC from a fluid-and-loop design into a repeatable operating practice.

slides.title: Operational Signals You Will Interpret
slides.subtitle: By the end of this module, you will be able to:
objectives: Prepare startup checks before fluid moves through the system.
Explain filling, purging, and non-condensable gas management.
Interpret the routine monitoring set called out in the OCP guideline.
Connect maintenance, leak response, deployment patterns, and lifecycle records.

slides.title: Startup Readiness Before Fluid Moves
slides.subtitle: A clean start reduces avoidable service issues later.

bullets.text: Keep the certificate of analysis for traceability of the received fluid.
The guideline calls out 5 degrees C to 35 degrees C ambient verification before startup.
Confirm correct installation, sealed and clean components, and completed safety and operational checks.
Keep opened components and fluid paths clean because contamination can affect loop behavior and service life.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf

figure.caption: Non-condensable gases can enter during service events; monitoring and purge provisions protect loop performance.
figure.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 980 360" style="width:100%;max-width:980px;height:auto;display:block;" role="img" aria-label="Filling and purging non-condensable gases in a two-phase loop">

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf

slides.title: The Monitoring Set That Tells the Truth
slides.subtitle: Pressure, temperature, and inventory signals make the two-phase loop observable.
rows: Whether vapor behavior matches expected boiling conditions.
Compare against theoretical vapor pressure for boiling temperature.
Thermal state of vapor returning from evaporators.
Watch for drift and poor condensation.
Liquid supply condition and subcooling margin.
Protect inlet conditions and thermal stability.
Fluid inventory and possible leak indication.
Track abnormal loss or level shift.
Purge is required per OCP guidance.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf
OCP Educational Webinar transcript and slides

slides.title: Maintenance and Leak Response Are Different
slides.subtitle: Dielectric two-phase loops change some maintenance concerns but do not eliminate service discipline.

bullets.text: The OCP guideline references filtration typically around 100 microns.
Dielectric loops reduce server-side water corrosion and biogrowth concerns, but compatibility and cleanliness remain important.
Buffer level, pressure behavior, and refrigerant sniffers can support leak detection.
A first indication does not automatically mean power down; follow the approved site response plan and supplier guidance.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf
2026 OCP EMEA Summit two-phase cooling panel transcript
OCP Educational Webinar transcript and slides

slides.title: Deployment Patterns and Decision Points
slides.subtitle: The same two-phase principle can appear in several infrastructure packages.

cards.title: Facility-water CDU
cards.body: Best where the site can connect to an FWS and wants familiar upstream rejection.

cards.title: Air-based sidecar
cards.body: Useful for pilots or sites without immediate liquid infrastructure access.

cards.title: In-rack air CDU
cards.body: A compact approach for constrained or staged deployments.

cards.title: External condenser
cards.body: Moves rejection outside the IT space and can support warmer operating conditions.

cards.title: Row or pod pilot
cards.body: Lets operators validate service workflow before fleet scale.

cards.title: Edge or brownfield
cards.body: Fit depends on space, power, water, service access, and reliability goals.

source_refs.source: 2026 OCP EMEA Summit two-phase cooling panel transcript
OCP Educational Webinar transcript and slides

slides.title: Lifecycle Records Make Scaling Auditable
slides.subtitle: Records turn one successful installation into a repeatable operating model.

bullets.text: Track source, certificate of analysis, and analysis results throughout use.
Record replaced components and materials of construction that contact the fluid.
Reuse, refurbishment, drain-back, and disposal should follow supplier guidance and local rules.
Audit trails help procurement, operations, safety, and sustainability teams compare deployments.

source_refs.source: Guidelines for Using Dielectric Heat Transfer Fluids in Two-Phase Cold Plate-Based Liquid-Cooled Racks-FINAL.pdf

slides.title: Course Operating Checklist
items: Start with clean, sealed components, retained fluid records, and verified operating conditions.
Monitor vapor pressure, vapor temperature, liquid pressure, liquid temperature, and buffer level as a set.
Use purge provisions when vapor-pressure behavior indicates non-condensables or an anomaly.
Select deployment patterns by site constraints, not by a one-size-fits-all claim.
Keep lifecycle records so fluid reuse, component changes, and disposal remain auditable.

source_refs.source: Internal course synthesis

slides.title: Check Your Operations Playbook
slides.subtitle: Answer once to continue. Correctness is not required, and you can retry.

questions.prompt: Which parameters are part of the routine monitoring set in the OCP guideline?

choices.text: Vapor pressure.
Vapor temperature.
Liquid pressure.
Liquid temperature.
Liquid level in the buffer.
Lighting level in the data hall.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. Pressure, temperature, and buffer level are the core operational signals.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The routine set is vapor pressure, vapor temperature, liquid pressure, liquid temperature, and buffer level.

questions.prompt: What does a greater-than-25-percent difference between measured vapor pressure and theoretical vapor pressure for a boiling temperature indicate in the OCP guideline?

choices.text: The rack has completed end-of-life disposal.
A purge is required because non-condensable gases or another anomaly may be present.
The system must immediately replace all cold plates.
The facility water system has changed from liquid to vapor.

questions.feedback_correct: Correct. The guideline uses that deviation as a condition that points to purging.
questions.feedback_incorrect: The guideline uses that deviation as a condition that points to purging so the system can return to designed operation.

source_refs.source: Internal course synthesis
